title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: package allows simulation convolution-closed count time series models cocoSim function. Model fitting performed cocoReg routine. passing cocoReg-type object, cocoForecast computes one-step ahead forecasting distribution. cocoBoot, cocoPit, cocoScore, cocoResid provide routines model assessment. main usage package illustrated within cocoReg function chapter. details examples functions see respective sections within vignette. default, functions make use RCPP implementation. However, users running Julia installation can choose call Julia background run functions specifying R function input. option particularly useful regression (), complex likelihood function must numerically evaluated obtain parameter estimates. leveraging Julia’s automatic differentiation capabilities, functions can take advantage numerical gradients, leading increased numerical stability faster convergence. Despite advantages, found Julia RCPP implementations produced qualitatively similar results tests. result, decided use RCPP implementation default option make package accessible non-Julia users.

title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: can install latest stable version coconots CRAN : latest version GitHub :
code: install.packages("coconots") devtools::install_github("manuhuth/coconots")

title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: Coconots runs RCPP Julia implementation. RCPP default. regression results can summarized using summary function.
code: library(coconots) length <- 500  pars <- c(1, 0.4, 0.2) set.seed(12345) data <- cocoSim(order = 1, type = "GP", par = pars,                 length = length) coco <- cocoReg(order = 1, type = "GP", data = data) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod': #>   method            from #>   as.zoo.data.frame zoo summary(coco) #> Coefficients: #>          Estimate   Std. Error         t #> lambda     1.0548       0.0930   11.3367 #> alpha      0.4201       0.0373   11.2508 #> eta        0.1609       0.0329    4.8958 #>  #> Type: GP  #> Order: 1  #>  #> Log-likelihood: -885.012

title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: order use Julia functions, need running version Julia installed. need install relevant Julia packages . installed, Julia implementation can addressed setting julia argument true. case need set seed within simulation function order pass Julia.
code: coconots::installJuliaPackages() #> Starting Julia ... library(coconots) length <- 500  pars <- c(1, 0.3, 0.01, 0.2) data <- cocoSim(order = 2, type = "Poisson", par = pars, length = length,                 julia = TRUE, julia_seed = 123) coco <- cocoReg(order = 2, type = "Poisson", data = data, julia = TRUE)

title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: provide different tools model assessment. One can even use RCPP implementation regression model assessment Julia implementation. desired, one needs specify output regression Julia compatible. Note necessary Julia option regression true.
code: library(coconots) length <- 500  pars <- c(1, 0.4) set.seed(12345) data <- cocoSim(order = 1, type = "Poisson", par = pars, length = length) coco <- cocoReg(order = 1, type = "Poisson", data = data,                 julia_installed=TRUE) (scores <- cocoScore(coco, julia = TRUE)) #> $log.score #> [1] 1.501935 #>  #> $quad.score #> [1] -0.2532058 #>  #> $rps.score #> [1] 0.619761 pit <- cocoPit(coco, julia = TRUE) plot(pit) boot <- cocoBoot(coco, julia = TRUE) plot(boot) forecast <- predict(coco, k=3, julia = TRUE) plot(forecast) #plot all k forecasts plot(forecast[[1]]) #plot density of one-step ahead forecast resid <- cocoResid(coco) plot(resid)

title: Convolution-Closed Models for Count Time Series
text: Model selection facilitated providing quick easy access scoring metrics.
code: library(coconots) length <- 500  pars <- c(1.3, 0.25, 0.03, 0.2, 0.3) set.seed(12345) data <- cocoSim(order = 2, type = "GP", par = pars, length = length) soc <- cocoSoc(data, julia=T) #> [1] "Computing PAR1 model." #> [1] "Computing PAR2 model." #> [1] "Computing GP1 model." #> [1] "Computing GP2 model." summary(soc) #>            Logarithmic Score   Quadratic Score   Ranked Probability Score #> 1   PAR1            2.265970        -0.1221436                   1.302572 #> 2   PAR2            2.264753        -0.1223451                   1.301770 #> 3    GP1            2.164318        -0.1363304                   1.263041 #> 4    GP2            2.161843        -0.1364925                   1.261362

title: Bootstrap Based Model Assessment Procedure — cocoBoot
text: Tsay, R. S. (1992) Model checking via parametric bootstraps time series analysis. Applied Statistics 41, 1–15.

title: Bootstrap Based Model Assessment Procedure — cocoBoot
code: lambda <- 1 alpha <- 0.4 set.seed(12345) data <- cocoSim(order = 1, type = "Poisson", par = c(lambda, alpha), length = 100) fit <- cocoReg(order = 1, type = "Poisson", data = data)  # bootstrap model assessment - R implementation boot_r <- cocoBoot(fit, rep.Bootstrap=400) plot(boot_r) #> Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0. #> ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`. #> ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the coconots package. #>   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/manuhuth/coconots/issues>.

title: Probability Integral Transform Based Model Assessment Procedure — cocoPit

what: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: function fits first- second-order (Generalized) Poisson integer autoregressive (G)PAR time series models count data discussed Jung Tremayne (2011). Autoregressive dependence past counts modeled using special random operator preserves integer values , closure convolution, ensures marginal distribution remains within family innovations. models can viewed stationary finite-order Markov chains, innovation distribution can either Poisson Generalized Poisson, latter accounting overdispersion. Estimation performed via maximum likelihood, option impose linear constraints. Without constraints, parameters may fall outside theoretically feasible space, optimization may faster. Method moments estimators used initialize numerical optimization, though custom starting values can provided. julia installed, users can opt run optimization julia potentially faster computation improved numerical stability via automatic differentiation. See details julia implementation.

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
code: cocoReg(   type,   order,   data,   xreg = NULL,   constrained.optim = TRUE,   b.beta = -10,   start = NULL,   start.val.adjust = TRUE,   method_optim = "Nelder-Mead",   replace.start.val = 1e-05,   iteration.start.val = 0.6,   method.hessian = "Richardson",   cores = 2,   julia = FALSE,   julia_installed = FALSE,   link_function = "log" )

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: type character, either "Poisson" "GP" indicating type innovation distribution order integer, either 1 2 indicating order model data time series data used analysis xreg optional matrix explanatory variables (without constant term) use regression model constrained.optim logical indicating whether optimization constrained, currently available R version b.beta numeric value indicating lower bound parameters explanatory variables optimization, currently available R version start optional numeric vector starting values optimization start.val.adjust logical indicating whether starting values adjusted, currently available R version method_optim character string indicating optimization method used, currently available R version. julia implementation default LBFGS algorithm replace.start.val numeric value indicating value replace invalid starting values, currently available R version iteration.start.val numeric value indicating proportion interval use new starting value, currently available R version method.hessian character string indicating method used approximate Hessian matrix, currently available R version cores numeric indicating number cores use, currently available R version (default: 2) julia TRUE, model estimated julia. can improve computational speed significantly since julia makes use derivatives using autodiff. case, type, order, data, xreg, start used inputs (default: FALSE). julia_installed TRUE, model R output contain julia compatible output element. link_function Specifies link function conditional mean innovation (\(\lambda\)). default log, available options include identity, relu, softplus. parameter applicable covariates used. Note using identity link function may result \(\lambda\) becoming negative. prevent , ensure covariates positive restrict parameter \(\beta\) positive values setting b.beta small positive value.

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: object class coco. contains parameter estimates, standard errors, log-likelihood, information model specifications. julia used parameter estimation julia installation parameter set TRUE, results contain additional Julia element called model julia assessment tools run julia implementation.

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: Let time series counts \(\{X_t\}\) \(R(\cdot)\) random operator differs model specifications. details random operator, see Jung Tremayne (2011) Joe (1996). general first-order model form $$X_t = R(X_{t-1}) + W_t,$$ general second-order model form $$X_t = R(X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}) + W_t,$$ \(W_t\) ..d Poisson (\(W_t \sim Po(\lambda_t)\)) Generalized Poisson (\(W_t \sim GP(\lambda_t, \eta)\)) innovations. closure convolution marginal distributions \(\{X_t\}\) therefore Poisson Generalized Poisson distributions, respectively. covariates used \(\lambda_t = \lambda\) covariates used $$g(\lambda_t) = \left(\beta_0 + \sum_{j = 1}^k \beta_j \cdot z_{t,j} \right),$$ whereby \(z_{t,j}\) \(j\)-th covariate time \(t\) \(g\) link function. Currently supported link functions : identity \(g(x) = x\), logarithmic link \(g(x) = \ln x\), relu \(g(x) = \max(x, \epsilon)\) small \(\epsilon > 0\), softplus \(g(x) = \log(\exp(x) - 1)\). ensure positivity \(\lambda\) identity function used, \(\beta_j, z_{t,j} > 0\) must enforced. Alternatively, computational values \(\lambda \leq 0\) can set small positive value. option named 'relu', due similarity ReLU function commonly used machine learning. softplus link uses numerically stable implementation inverse \(\log(1 + \exp(x))\), ensuring \(\lambda > 0\) smooth gradients everywhere. Standard errors computed square root diagonal elements inverse Hessian. function implemented two versions. default runs RCPP. alternative version uses julia implementation can chosen setting argument julia TRUE. order use feature, running julia installation required system. RCPP implementation uses derivative-free Nelder-Mead optimizer obtain parameter estimates. julia implementation makes use julia's automatic differentiation order obtain gradients can use LBFGS algorithm optimization. enhances numeric stability optimization yields internal validation methods yield qualitatively parameter estimates. Furthermore, julia implementation can increase computational speed significantly, especially large models. model assessment tools cocoBoot, cocoPit, cocoScore use julia implementation well, cocoReg run julia. Additionally, one can make RCPP output cocoReg compatible julia model assessments setting julia_installed true. case, user can choose RCPP julia implementation model assessment.

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: Jung, R. C. Tremayne, . R. (2011) Convolution-closed models count time series applications. Journal Time Series Analysis, 32, 268–280. Joe, H. (1996) Time series models univariate margins convolution-closed infinitely divisible class. Journal Applied Probability, 33, 664–677.

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
text: Manuel Huth

title: Fitting First- and Second Order (G)PAR Models — cocoReg
code: ## GP2 model without covariates length <- 1000 par <- c(0.5,0.2,0.05,0.3,0.3) data <- cocoSim(order = 2, type = "GP", par = par, length = length) fit <- cocoReg(order = 2, type = "GP", data = data) #> Warning: The initial value of alpha2 is not in the feasible region and was set to 1e-05   ##Poisson1 model with covariates length <- 1000 period <- 12 sin <- sin(2*pi/period*(1:length)) cos <- cos(2*pi/period*(1:length)) cov <- cbind(sin, cos) par <- c(0.2, 0.2, -0.2) data <- cocoSim(order = 1, type = "Poisson", par = par, xreg = cov, length = length) #> Warning: No burn-in period is specified using the init argument. Hence, the resulting simulated time series might not be stationary. You can add a custom burn-in period by passing it to the init argument. This could be, for example, done by simulating a burn-in period with appropriate covariates using cocoSim and passing the resulting time series to the init argument of a new cocoSim run. fit <- cocoReg(order = 1, type = "Poisson", data = data, xreg = cov)

title: Scoring Rule Based Model Assessment Procedure — cocoScore
text: Scoring rules assign numerical score based predictive distribution observed data  measure quality probabilistic predictions. provided model selection tool computed averages relevant set (-sample) predictions. Scoring rules , generally, negatively oriented penalties one seeks minimize. literature developed large number scoring rules , unless unique clearly defined underlying decision problem, automatic choice (proper) scoring rule used given situation. Therefore, use variety scoring rules may appropriate take advantage specific emphases strengths. Three proper scoring rules (definition concept propriety see Gneiting Raftery, 2007), Jung, McCabe Tremayne (2016) found particularly useful, implemented. information see references listed .

title: Scoring Rule Based Model Assessment Procedure — cocoScore

(2007) Strictly proper scoring rules, prediction, estimation. Journal American Statistical Association, 102:359-378. Jung, R. C., McCabe, B.P.M. Tremayne, .R. (2016). Model validation diagnostics. Handbook Discrete Valued Time Series. Edited Davis, R.., Holan, S.H., Lund, R. Ravishanker, N.. Boca Raton: Chapman Hall, pp. 189–218. Jung, R. C. Tremayne, . R. title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
code: # S3 method for class 'coco' predict(   object,   k = 1,   number_simulations = 1000,   alpha = 0.05,   simulate_one_step_ahead = FALSE,   max = NULL,   epsilon = 1e-08,   xcast = NULL,   decimals = 4,   julia = FALSE,   ... )

title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
text: object object fitted previously, class coco. k number steps ahead forecast computed (Default: 1). number_simulations number simulation runs compute (Default: 1000). alpha Significance level used construct prediction intervals (Default: 0.05). simulate_one_step_ahead FALSE, one-step ahead prediction obtained using analytical predictive distribution. TRUE, bootstrapping used. max maximum number forecast support plot. NULL values cumulative distribution function 1- epsilon used plot. epsilon max NULL, epsilon determines range support used subsequent automatic plotting using R's plot() function. xcast optional matrix covariate values forecasting. NULL, function assumes covariates. decimals Number decimal places forecast probabilities julia TRUE, estimate predicted julia (Default: FALSE). ... Optional arguments.

title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
text: cocoForecastCollection object (list cocoForecast objects, one per step ahead). Use summary() obtain data frame point forecasts (mean, median, mode) prediction intervals. Individual steps accessible via forecast[[]].

title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
text: Returns forecasts mass point k-step ahead distribution fitted model. exact predictive distributions one-step ahead predictions models included provided Jung Tremayne (2011), maximum likelihood estimates replace true model parameters. k>1 forecast distributions estimated using parametric bootstrap. See Jung Tremanye (2006). --sample values covariates can provided, necessary. k > 1

title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
text: Jung, R.C. Tremayne, . R. (2011) Convolution-closed models count time series applications. Journal Time Series Analysis, 32, 3, 268–280. Jung, R.C. Tremayne, .R. (2006) Coherent forecasting integer time series models. International Journal Forecasting 22, 223–238

title: K-Step Ahead Forecast Distributions — predict.coco
code: length <- 500 pars <- c(1, 0.4) set.seed(12345) data <- cocoSim(order = 1, type = "Poisson", par = pars, length = length) fit <- cocoReg(order = 1, type = "Poisson", data = data) forecast <- predict(fit, k=1, simulate_one_step_ahead = FALSE) plot(forecast[[1]]) #plot one-step ahead forecast distribution

title: coconots 2.0.3
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-14 Added softplus link function conditional mean innovation rate cocoReg() (covariate models). Uses numerically stable formulation, smooth everywhere, guarantees positive rate. Added S3 summary methods forecast objects (cocoForecast, cocoForecastCollection) returning data frame point forecasts (mean, median, mode) prediction intervals per forecast horizon.

title: coconots 2.0.1
text: CRAN release: 2025-07-24 Added NEWS.md file track changes package. Added new S3 methods object created cocoReg.
